content: Let a spherical block of ice at $-7^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ be exposed to atmospheric air at $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ with the gravitational direction as shown in the figure below. What will be the overall direction of air flow in this situation?
explanation: Air close to the Ice Ball will go in downward direction becouse of weight is more than Buoyant force because $T_{\text {iceball }}$ is less.

content: A plane, solid slab of thickness L, shown in the figure, has thermal conductivity k that varies with the spatial coordinate x as k = A + Bx, where A and B are positive constants (A > 0, B > 0). The slab walls are maintained at fixed temperatures of T(x = 0) = 0 and T(x = L) = T0 > 0. The slab has no internal heat sources. Considering one-dimensional heat transfer, which one of the following plots qualitatively depicts the steady-state temperature distribution within the slab?

Consider a laterally insulated rod of length L and constant thermal conductivity. Assuming one-dimensional heat conduction in the rod, which of the following steady-state temperature profile(s) can occur without internal heat generation?
explanation:
This is heat flow through constant cross - sectional area. Without volumetric heat Generation. So only linear temperature profile is possible.

Hence, the correct options are (A) & (B).

content: A pipe of $$25$$ $$mm$$ outer diameter carries steam. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

options.content: The outer radius of the pipe is equal to the critical radius.
The outer radius of the pipe is less than the critical radius.
Adding the insulation will reduce the heat loss.
Adding the insulation will increase the heat loss.

content: Thermal conductivity is lower for

Water enters a tube of diameter, $D=60 \mathrm{~mm}$ with mass flow rate of $0.01 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ as shown in the figure below. The inlet mean temperature is $T_{m, i}=293 \mathrm{~K}$ and the uniform heat flux at the surface of the tube is $2000 \mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$. For the exit mean temperature of $T_{m, o}=$ 353 K , the length of the tube, $L$ is ___________ m (rounded off to 1 decimal place). Use the specific heat of water as $4181 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$.
explanation:
Given: $\mathrm{D}=60 \mathrm{~mm}=0.060$ meter, $T_{b m(\text { inlet })}=293 \mathrm{~K}, T_{b m(\text { outlet })}=353 \mathrm{~K}$, $\mathrm{q}^{\prime \prime}=2000 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^2, \dot{m}=0.01 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{s}, C_p=4180$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Total rate of heat transfe: }\\ &\begin{aligned} q & =q^{\prime \prime}(\pi \mathrm{DL})=\dot{m} C_p\left(T_{\text {bm(outlet) }}-T_{\text {bm(inlet) }}\right) \\ 2000 \times \pi \times 0.060 \times l & =0.01 \times 4180 \times(353-293) \\ \Rightarrow \quad l & =6.6526 \text { meter } \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: Consider a slab of 20 mm thickness. There is a uniform heat generation of $ \dot{q} = 100 \text{ MW/m}^3 $ inside the slab. The left and right faces of the slab are maintained at 150 °C and 110 °C, respectively. The plate has a constant thermal conductivity of 200 W/(m.K). Considering a 1-D steady state heat conduction, the location of the maximum temperature from the left face will be at ______mm (answer in integer).

Consider steady state, one-dimensional heat conduction in an infinite slab of thickness 2L (L = 1 m) as shown in the figure. The conductivity (k) of the material varies with temperature as k = CT, where T is the temperature in K, and C is a constant equal to 2 W.m-1K-2. There is a uniform heat generation of 1280 kW/m3 in the slab. If both faces of the slab are maintained at 600 K, then the temperature at x = 0 is _______ K (in integer).
explanation:
Thickness of slab: $$2L (L = 1 \text{ m})$$

Conductivity: $$k = CT$$

Where $$C = 2 \text{ W·m}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-2}$$

Uniform heat generation: $$q = 1280 \text{ kW/m}^3$$

Temperature at both faces: $$T = 600 \text{ K}$$

Steady-State, One-Dimensional Heat Conduction

The governing heat conduction equation is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) = -q$$

Integrating this equation:

$$k \frac{dT}{d x} = -qx + C_1$$

Substituting $$k = 2T$$ gives:

$$2T \frac{dT}{d x} = -qx + C_1$$

Rearranging and integrating:

$$T \frac{dT}{d x} = \frac{-qx + C_1}{2} dx$$

$$\frac{T^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{-qx^2}{2} + C_1x + C_2\right) \quad \text{(i)}$$

Boundary Conditions

At $$x = L = 1 \text{ m}$$, $$T = 600 \text{ K}$$. Substitute these into the equation (i):

$$600^2 = \frac{-1280 \times 10^3 \times 1}{2} + C_1 \times 1 + C_2$$

$$C_1 + C_2 = 10^6 \quad \text{(ii)}$$

At $$x = -L = -1 \text{ m}$$, $$T = 600 \text{ K}$$:

$$600^2 = \frac{-1280 \times 10^3 \times (-1)}{2} + C_1 \times (-1) + C_2$$

$$-C_1 + C_2 = 10^6 \quad \text{(iii)}$$

From equations (ii) and (iii), solving for $$C_1$$ and $$C_2$$:

$$C_1 = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad C_2 = 10^6$$

Solving for the Temperature at x = 0

Using equation (i):

$$T^2 = 10^6 - \frac{qx^2}{2}$$

At $$x = 0$$:

$$[T(0)]^2 = 10^6$$

Therefore,

$$T(0) = \sqrt{10^6} = 1000 \text{ K}$$

Hence, the temperature at $$x = 0$$ is 1000 K.

content:
Consider a rod of uniform thermal conductivity whose one end (x = 0) is insulated and the other end (x = L) is exposed to flow of air at temperature T∞ with convective heat transfer coefficient h. The cylindrical surface of the rod is insulated so that the heat transfer is strictly along the axis of the rod. The rate of internal heat generation per unit volume inside the rod is given as 

$\rm \dot q = \cos \frac{2 \pi x}{L}$

The steady-state temperature at the mid-location of the rod is given as TA. What will be the temperature at the same location, if the convective heat transfer coefficient increases to 2h?

options.content: $T_A \left( 1 - \frac{\dot q L}{4 \pi h} \right) + \frac{\dot q L}{4 \pi h} T_\infty$

explanation:
General heat conduction equation for a plane wall :

$$ \nabla^2 T + \frac{q_g}{k} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \frac{dT}{d\tau} $$

For one-dimensional steady-state heat conduction :

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{k} = 0 \\\\ & \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = \frac{-\dot{q}_g}{k} \end{aligned} $$

Integrating with respect to $x$ :

$$ \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{-\dot{q}_g}{k}x + C_1 \quad \text{............(A)}$$

Integrating again :

$$ T = \frac{-\dot{q}_g}{2k} x^2 + C_1 x + C_2 \quad \text{............(B)}$$

Here, $C_1$ and $C_2$ are integration constants.

Boundary conditions :

At $x = 0$, $\dot{Q} = 0$

At $x = \delta$, $T = T_w$

Applying the first boundary condition :

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text{(1) At } x = 0, \dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = 0, \\\\ & -kA \frac{dT}{dx} = 0, \\\\ & \frac{dT}{dx} = 0, \\\\ & \left. \frac{dT}{dx} \right|_{x=0} = 0 \end{aligned} $$

From equation (A) :

$$ C_1 = 0 $$

Applying the second boundary condition :

At $x = \delta$, $T = T_w$ [Substitute in equation (B)]

$$ \begin{aligned} & T_w = \frac{-\dot{q}_g}{2k} \delta^2 + C_2 \\ & C_2 = T_w + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{2k} \delta^2 \end{aligned} $$

Substituting the values of $C_1$ and $C_2$ in equation (B) :

$$ T = T_w + \frac{\dot{q}_g}{2k} \left( \delta^2 - x^2 \right) $$

The temperature variation follows a parabolic distribution.

From the problem statement :

$$ T_A = T_w + \frac{\cos \left( \frac{2 \pi x}{L} \right)}{2k} \left( \delta^2 - x^2 \right) $$

(Since $\dot{q}_g = \cos \left( \frac{2 \pi x}{L} \right)$ when $h = H$)

At $ \delta = \frac{x}{2} $ (mid-plane) :

$$ T = T_w + \frac{\cos \left( \frac{2 \pi x}{L} \right)}{2k} \left( \frac{x^2}{4} - x^2 \right) \quad \text{............(C)}$$

Equation (C) demonstrates that the temperature distribution is independent of the heat transfer coefficient.

Hence, the heat transfer coefficient does not affect the temperature $T_A$.

Therefore, the correct option is $ \text{C} $.

content: Consider a solid slab (thermal conductivity, k = 10 W∙m-1∙K-1) with thickness 0.2 m and of infinite extent in the other two directions as shown in the figure. Surface 2, at 300 K, is exposed to a fluid flow at a free stream temperature (T∞) of 293 K, with a convective heat transfer coefficient (h) of 100 W∙m-2∙K-1. Surface 2 is opaque, diffuse and gray with an emissivity (ε) of 0.5 and exchanges heat by radiation with very large surroundings at 0 K. Radiative heat transfer inside the solid slab is neglected. The Stefan-Boltzmann constant is 5.67 × 10-8 W∙m-2∙K-4. The temperature T1 of Surface 1 of the slab, under steady-state conditions, is _________ K (round off to the nearest integer).
explanation:
Consider a solid slab with the following properties: thermal conductivity (k) of 10 W∙m-1∙K-1, thickness of 0.2 m, and infinite extent in the other two directions. Refer to the figure below.

We have the following conditions:

  • Temperature at Surface 2 (T2): 300 K
  • Free stream temperature (T∞): 293 K
  • Convective heat transfer coefficient (h): 100 W∙m-2∙K-1
  • Emissivity of Surface 2 (ε): 0.5
  • Temperature of the large surroundings (Tlarge): 0 K
  • Stefan-Boltzmann constant (σ): 5.67 × 10-8 W∙m-2∙K-4

The energy balance equation is given by:

$ Q_{\text {convection}} = Q_{\text {conduction}} + Q_{\text {radiation}} $

The equation can be expressed as:

$ \frac{k A (T_1 - T_2)}{L} = h A (T_2 - T_{\infty}) + \varepsilon \sigma A (T_2^4 - T_{\text {large}}^4) $

Substituting the given values:

$ \frac{10 \times (T_1 - 300)}{0.2} = 100 \times (300 - 293) + 0.5 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (300^4 - 0^4) $

Solving the above equation, we get:

$ T_1 = 318.59 \, \text{K} $

Thus, the temperature T1 of Surface 1 of the slab, under steady-state conditions, is approximately 318.59 K.

content:
Steam is flowing through an insulated steel pipe shown in the fig, is losing heat to the surroundings. The details are as follows

Inner radius of steel pipe $$=50mm,$$
Outer radius of the steel pipe $$=57mm,$$
Outer radius of insulation $$=157 mm,$$
Thermal conductivity of steel $$=43$$ $$W/mK$$
Thermal conductivity of insulating material $$=0.1$$ $$W/mK$$
Heat transfer coefficient on steam side $$ = 570W/{m^2}K$$
Heat transfer coefficient on air side $$ = 12W/{m^2}K,$$
Temperature of steam $$ = {500^ \circ }C$$
Temperature of surroundings $$ = {30^ \circ }C$$

Calculate the heat loss per meter length of pipe and temp of the outer surface of the insulation.
